text:
There once was a little garden. Not a sprawling expanse, but a cozy plot nestled in a small town park. Every year, flowers bloomed in unexpected patterns, and birds made their homes among the vines. The garden was famous among the townsfolk—not for its beauty, which was modest, but for its annual tradition.

Every autumn, a little old lady named Clara would come and organize a pumpkin-carving contest. She'd invite a handful of folks, and they'd spend afternoons carving pumpkins together. The competition wasn't fierce; they rarely won. Rather, it was all about the fun.

One particular autumn was rainy, as falls tended to be in that part of the year. The little old lady sat at her stool, surrounded by laughing friends, as she worked on her enormous pumpkin. She knew this would be her last year—old age had joined forces with a bad knee.

Her pumpkin wasn't perfect. It had three holes that the children had carved faces into
